title: Low Pressure Lockout
shortDescription: The system detected low refrigerant pressure and locked out.
fullDescription: Trane diagnostic code 126 indicates the low pressure switch tripped, typically due to low refrigerant charge from a leak or a restricted metering device.
safetyWarning: Refrigerant handling requires EPA certification. Do not attempt to add refrigerant. Turn off the system to prevent compressor damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Rule out airflow problems by replacing the indoor filter.

fixSteps.title: Check for Ice
fixSteps.instruction: Look for ice on refrigerant lines or the indoor coil. If iced, run fan-only to thaw.

fixSteps.title: Look for Leak Signs
fixSteps.instruction: Check for oil stains at refrigerant connections.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: A tech must find and repair the leak, then recharge the system.

whenToCallPro: For any low pressure code. Refrigerant issues need professional service.

faq.q: Does the system need more refrigerant?
faq.a: Probably, but adding refrigerant without fixing the leak is a temporary fix that wastes money.

faq.q: How much does a leak repair cost?
faq.a: $200-$1,500 depending on location.

faq.q: Can I run the AC with low refrigerant?
faq.a: No, it can damage the compressor.

title: High Pressure Lockout
shortDescription: High refrigerant pressure detected — system locked out.
fullDescription: Trane code 79 indicates the high pressure switch tripped due to excessive head pressure, usually from restricted airflow at the condenser or overcharged refrigerant.
safetyWarning: High pressure in the refrigerant system can be dangerous. Do not open any refrigerant connections. Turn off the system.
symptoms: AC shuts off during hot days

fixSteps.title: Clean Condenser
fixSteps.instruction: Spray outdoor coils with a garden hose from inside out.

fixSteps.title: Check Fan
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the outdoor fan spins and runs.

fixSteps.title: Replace Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Change the indoor filter.

fixSteps.title: Clear Around Unit
fixSteps.instruction: Maintain 2+ feet clearance around outdoor unit.

whenToCallPro: If cleaning and fan check do not resolve the issue.

faq.a: High ambient temps make the condenser work harder, especially with dirty coils.
At least annually, ideally before summer.
Most Trane units auto-reset after pressure drops.

title: Outdoor Coil Sensor Failure
shortDescription: The outdoor coil temperature sensor has failed or is reading incorrectly.
fullDescription: Trane code 91 indicates the outdoor coil temperature sensor (thermistor) has failed, is disconnected, or is reading out of range.
safetyWarning: Turn off the system at the disconnect before inspecting any wiring on the outdoor unit.
symptoms: System may not start or operates abnormally
Heat pump may not defrost properly

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the outdoor unit for 2 minutes and restart.

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Wire
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the sensor wire connection at the control board for looseness or damage.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure thermistor resistance with a multimeter. Compare to the temperature/resistance chart for your model.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If resistance is out of spec, replace the thermistor. Match the exact part for your model.

whenToCallPro: If sensor replacement does not fix the code.

faq.a: It measures the outdoor coil temperature for defrost control and system optimization.
$10-$40 for the part.

faq.q: Can the system run without it?
faq.a: Some systems will run in a limited mode, but defrost and efficiency will be affected.

title: Overcurrent at Compressor
shortDescription: The compressor is drawing excessive current.
fullDescription: Code 76 indicates the compressor is pulling more amps than the protection allows, suggesting a mechanical or electrical problem.
safetyWarning: High current can cause fire. Turn off the system at the disconnect immediately. Do not keep restarting.

fixSteps.title: Turn Off
fixSteps.instruction: Shut off at the disconnect to prevent fire risk.

fixSteps.title: Clean Condenser
fixSteps.instruction: Clean outdoor coils to reduce load.

fixSteps.title: Wait Before Retry
fixSteps.instruction: Wait 30 minutes, restart once. If it trips again, stop.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: Tech must check amp draw, capacitor, and compressor windings.

faq.a: If the capacitor failed, yes. A new capacitor costs $100-$250 installed.

faq.q: Does this mean I need a new AC?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Failed capacitors are cheap to fix. Even compressor replacement may be covered by warranty.
Yes, overcurrent can cause electrical fires. Do not ignore this code.

title: High Discharge Temperature
shortDescription: Compressor discharge temperature exceeded the safe limit.
fullDescription: Code 89 indicates the compressor is overheating, typically from low refrigerant, dirty coils, or a failing compressor.
safetyWarning: Turn off the AC to prevent compressor damage. The discharge line can be extremely hot.

fixSteps.title: Turn Off
fixSteps.instruction: Stop the system to protect the compressor.

fixSteps.title: Clean Condenser
fixSteps.instruction: Clean outdoor coils after unit cools.

fixSteps.title: Check Fan
fixSteps.instruction: Verify condenser fan runs.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: Tech must check charge and compressor health.

whenToCallPro: If cleaning does not fix it.

faq.a: Yes, it can cause permanent damage to the compressor windings.
Often yes. Low refrigerant makes the compressor run hotter.
Turn off the AC and get it checked within a day or two.

title: Communication Loss with Thermostat
shortDescription: The outdoor unit lost communication with the Trane thermostat.
fullDescription: Code 23 on a Trane system indicates a communication link failure between the outdoor unit and the indoor thermostat or air handler.
safetyWarning: Turn off both indoor and outdoor units before inspecting communication wiring.
symptoms: Outdoor unit may run continuously or not at all

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off both indoor and outdoor units for 5 minutes, then restart.

fixSteps.title: Check Wire
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect communication wire between indoor and outdoor units.

fixSteps.title: Verify Thermostat
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the thermostat is compatible with your Trane system. XL and XR series need matching thermostats.

fixSteps.title: Call Trane Dealer
fixSteps.instruction: Communicating system issues often need dealer-level tools.

whenToCallPro: If power cycling and wire checks do not restore communication.

faq.a: Trane communicating systems require compatible Trane thermostats. Non-communicating systems can use standard thermostats.

faq.q: Is this common after power outages?
faq.a: Yes, power surges can disrupt communication. Power cycling usually fixes it.

faq.q: How much is a Trane thermostat?
faq.a: Trane communicating thermostats range from $200-$500.

title: Indoor Coil Sensor Failure
shortDescription: The indoor coil temperature sensor has failed.
fullDescription: Code 87 indicates the indoor evaporator coil thermistor is reading out of range or has failed.
safetyWarning: Turn off the system before working on the indoor unit.
symptoms: System may not protect against freezing
May run continuously or not at all

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Restart the system.

fixSteps.title: Check Connection
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the sensor wire is plugged in at the control board.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure thermistor resistance and compare to spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace
fixSteps.instruction: If out of spec, replace with OEM sensor.

whenToCallPro: If replacement does not resolve the code.

faq.a: Monitors evaporator coil temp to prevent freezing and optimize operation.
$10-$40 for the sensor.
Not recommended — freeze protection will not work.

title: Compressor Contactor Open / Not Engaging
shortDescription: The compressor contactor failed to close, preventing the compressor from starting.
fullDescription: Code 3 on some Trane systems indicates the compressor contactor is not engaging. The contactor is an electrically operated switch that powers the compressor.
safetyWarning: High voltage is present at the contactor. Always turn off the disconnect before inspecting.
symptoms: Outdoor fan runs but compressor does not

fixSteps.title: Listen for Click
fixSteps.instruction: When the thermostat calls for cooling, listen for a click from the outdoor unit. No click means the contactor is not engaging.

fixSteps.title: Visual Inspection
fixSteps.instruction: With power OFF at the disconnect, inspect the contactor for burnt/pitted contacts or melted plastic.

fixSteps.title: Check 24V Signal
fixSteps.instruction: Verify 24VAC is reaching the contactor coil terminals during a cooling call.

fixSteps.title: Replace Contactor
fixSteps.instruction: A worn contactor should be replaced. This is a relatively inexpensive part but involves high voltage wiring.

whenToCallPro: For contactor replacement — involves high voltage wiring.

faq.a: An electrically operated switch that turns the compressor and condenser fan on and off.
$20-$50 for the part; $100-$300 installed.
5-10 years. They wear out from repeated switching cycles.

title: Compressor Locked Rotor — Failed to Start
shortDescription: The compressor could not start and is in a locked rotor condition.
fullDescription: Code 185 indicates the compressor motor stalled or could not begin rotation, drawing extreme amperage. This is a serious condition that requires immediate attention.
safetyWarning: Turn off the disconnect immediately. A locked rotor can cause breaker trips, electrical damage, or fire.

causes.cause: Liquid slugging from improper refrigerant migration

fixSteps.title: Disconnect Power
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the outdoor disconnect immediately.

fixSteps.title: Single Retry
fixSteps.instruction: Wait 30 minutes, restart once. If it fails again, stop.

fixSteps.title: Do Not Continue
fixSteps.instruction: If the compressor will not start after one retry, do not keep attempting.

fixSteps.title: Professional Required
fixSteps.instruction: Tech must test capacitor, compressor windings, and attempt hard start.

faq.a: Sometimes. It provides extra starting torque. Costs $100-$250 installed.
Not necessarily. A failed capacitor can cause locked rotor symptoms and is much cheaper to fix.
